coredata.dc:title: Thermal and optical behavior of electrochromic windows fabricated with carbon-based counterelectrode
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 1999 Elsevier Science Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Nishikitani, Yoshinori
Asano, Tsuyoshi
Uchida, Soichi
Kubo, Takaya
coredata.dc:description: We proposed a carbon-based counterelectrode for electrochromic windows (ECWs) and fabricated a new solid-state ECW consisting of an indium tin oxide electrode (ITO, IN2O3:SN)/a WO3 film/a polymeric solid electrolyte (PSE)/a carbon-based counterelectrode. The carbon-based counterelectrode is a series of arrays of carbon material dots formed on an ITO substrate and is virtually transparent in a visible region, Those carbon dots play a part in the formation of an electric double layer in an electrochromic reaction of the ECW. The electric double layer capacitance of the counterelectrode increases linearly as a function of carbon-dot covering percentage on the ITO substrate. Maximum differential optical density of the ECW increases with the covering percentage of the carbon dots up to a point and levels off for further increase in the covering percentage. The response time of coloration decreases with temperatures, which is caused chiefly by the temperature dependence of an ionic conductivity of the PSE. The behavior of ECWs is explained well with a simple equivalent-circuit comprising two capacitors corresponding to the WO3, film and the carbon-based counterelectrode, an electric resistor of the PSE and a power source connected in series.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid-state electrochromic window
Tungsten oxide
Polymeric solid electrolyte
Carbon-based counterelectrode
Equivalent-circuit analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of temperature dependence of electrochromic switching behavior

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic cross sections of (a) the ECW and (b) the EDLC.
Equivalent-circuits for (a) the ECW and (b) the EDLC; C ECW and C EDLC are capacitance, respectively, of the ECW and the EDLC. R ECW is resistance of the ECW and REDLC is that of the EDLC.
Cyclic voltammogram of the ECW; The cyclic voltammogram was measured at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1.
ΔOD of the ECW monitored at 633 nm; a coloring potential is −1.5 V and a bleaching potential is 1.0 V.
Electric double layer capacitance of the carbon-based counterelectrode as a function of the covering percentage of the carbon-based material on the ITO substrate.
Relation between ΔODmax, and covering percentage of the carbon-based material on the ITO substrate.
Response time of the ECW measured in a temperature region between −20°C and 80°C; τ is the time required to obtain the coloration ratio of 4.
Temperature dependence of resistance of (a) the ECW and (b) the EDLC.
Temperature dependence of capacitance of (a) the ECW and (b) the EDLC.

head.ce:title: Thermal and optical behavior of electrochromic windows fabricated with carbon-based counterelectrode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nippon Oil Company, Ltd, Central Technical Research Laboratory, 8, Chidori-cho, Naka-ku, Yokohama 231-0815, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We proposed a carbon-based counterelectrode for electrochromic windows (ECWs) and fabricated a new solid-state ECW consisting of an indium tin oxide electrode (ITO, IN2O3:SN)/a WO3 film/a polymeric solid electrolyte (PSE)/a carbon-based counterelectrode. The carbon-based counterelectrode is a series of arrays of carbon material dots formed on an ITO substrate and is virtually transparent in a visible region, Those carbon dots play a part in the formation of an electric double layer in an electrochromic reaction of the ECW. The electric double layer capacitance of the counterelectrode increases linearly as a function of carbon-dot covering percentage on the ITO substrate. Maximum differential optical density of the ECW increases with the covering percentage of the carbon dots up to a point and levels off for further increase in the covering percentage. The response time of coloration decreases with temperatures, which is caused chiefly by the temperature dependence of an ionic conductivity of the PSE. The behavior of ECWs is explained well with a simple equivalent-circuit comprising two capacitors corresponding to the WO3, film and the carbon-based counterelectrode, an electric resistor of the PSE and a power source connected in series.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochromism is a unique property of materials that change color reversibly in response to an applied external potential. It dates back to 1969 that the literature published by Deb [1] triggered the boom of research works on electrochromism in academic and technological fields. Since then, much effort has been put into the development of electrochromic devices such as display panels, antiglare car rear-view mirrors, and electrochromic windows (ECWs). In particular, ECWs are of great interest among them from the viewpoints of energy conservation. Because ECWs can control heat and light entering and/or leaving form buildings when they are controlled appropriately. An ECW comprises three primary components: (1) an electrochromic working electrode, (2) an electrically conductive counterelectrode and (3) an electrolyte. In the past decade, various inorganic materials have been examined as an electrochromic working electrode for ECWs, but a tungsten oxide (WO3) film, a cathodically coloring material, is the best inorganic electrochromic compound [2,3] in that it has a high coloration efficiency and achieves long-term electrochromic stability. An ECW consisting of a WO3 working electrode requires an optically transparent, electrochemically reversible, and charge balanced counterelectrode, which acts as an ion reservoir. In spite of many attempts to explore a material satisfying completely all the essential requirements of a counterelectrode, they appear to be still unsuccessful.

We proposed a carbon-based counterelectrode for an ECW and fabricated a new solid-state ECW [4]; an indium tin oxide electrode (ITO, In2O3:Sn)/a WO3 film/a polymeric solid electrolyte (PSE)/a carbon-based counterelectrode. The carbon-based counterelectrode is a series of arrays of carbon material dots formed on an ITO substrate and is virtually transparent in a visible region when the covering percentage of the carbon materials is below 30%. The carbon-based counterelectrode shows a large electrostatic capacitance in a PSE and acts as an electric double layer capacitor (EDLC). In forming the electric double layer, no chemical reactions occur, causing any structural change in the carbon-based material, and the electric double layer can store both negative and positive charges. Hence, the carbon-based counterelectrode expected to gain high electrochemical stability, and is one of the prime candidates for a counterelectrode of the ECW. Actually we are successful in operating the ECW in the size of 10×10 cm at 80°C through 100,000 successive coloring and bleaching cycles (coloration ratio is 4) without reaching a point of failure. The ECW is, therefore, promising for solar energy control in architectural, automotive and aircraft applications, where great durability is required for ECWs.

The purpose of this paper is following three things. (1) Electrochemical properties of the ECW and the EDLC are examined by applying an equivalent-circuit analysis to obtain their resistance and capacitance. (2) The temperature dependence of the resistance and capacitance in both cells are observed to elucidate a leading factor that determines the temperature dependence of the ECW. (3) The advantages of employing the carbon-based electrode are verified.

WO3 films were deposited on ITO substrates (10 Ω/□) with a tungsten target by means of reactive DC magnetron sputtering. Mixture of argon and oxygen (ratio of 1 to 3) were fed into a sputtering chamber, and the pressure inside the chamber was raised to 3.0 Pa. The 550-nm thick WO3 films were deposited on ITO substrates in the size of 5×5 cm with discharge power density of 1.2 W cm−2. Carbon-based paste consists of activated carbon powders, which have a specific surface area more than 2000 m2 g−1, and thermal curable binder. Five different covering percentages of carbon-based counterelectrodes (11, 17, 23, 29 and 35%) were formed by screen-printing the carbon-based paste in a form of uniformly arrayed dots on an ITO substrate. The carbon-based counterelectrode thus formed was thermally treated at 180°C. The experimental procedures were described elsewhere in more detail [5,6].

The cross-section of the ECW fabricated with the carbon-based counterelectrode is schematically depicted in Fig. 1(a). The EDLC was also assembled from two carbon-based counterelectrodes with same covering percentage as shown in Fig. 1(b). A PSE placed between two electrodes is a gel electrolyte composed of γ-butyrolactone (GBL), LiClO4 and cross-linked methacrylated polyethylene oxide.

Cyclic voltammometry of the ECW was carried out with a potentiostat (EG&G, PARC273). Potential applied to the ECW was swept between −1.5 and 1.0 V at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1. The differential optical density (ΔOD) of the ECW was monitored with a 633 nm-beam of He/Ne laser by applying the coloring potential of −1.5 V and the bleaching potential of 1.0 V across the ECW. ΔOD is defined as −log(T c/T b), where T c and T b are transmittance, respectively, of a colored and a bleached state. Maximum differential optical density (ΔODmax) was obtained when ΔOD levels off and was almost independent of further elapse of potential application time. The temperature dependence of discharging behavior of the EDLC and ΔOD of the ECW were measured in a temperature region between −20 and 80°C in a temperature-controlled chamber. In the chamber, the ECW was colored until ΔOD reached 0.7, and then bleached back to an initial transparent state to obtain the temperature dependence of coloration response time.

The electrochromic process of the ECW fabricated with the carbon-based counterelectrode can be summarized by the reaction scheme:
(1)
WO3
(transparent)+xLi+ +xe− ⇄LixWO3
(blue)
(2)
C+ClO− 4 ⇄C+ ⋯ClO− 4 +e− ,
where C is a carbon material playing a part in the formation of an electric double layer as expressed by C+⋯ClO4 −.

DC electrochemical switching behavior of the ECW and the EDLC are assumed to be represented by equivalent-circuits composed of a resistor, a capacitor, and a power source as given in Fig. 2(a) and (b) [7,8]. The resistor is the sum of resistance of electrolyte, working electrode and counterelectrode, charge transfer resistance, and mass-transfer resistance. The capacitor is the sum of working electrode capacitance and counterelectrode capacitance. The capacitance of the ECW (C ECW) and that of the EDLC (C EDLC) are given by
(3) C ECW = C WO 3 ×C carbon
C WO 3 +C carbon
(4) C EDLC = C carbon
2 ,
where C WO3 and C carbon is the capacitance, respectively, of a WO3 film and a carbon-based counterelectrode.

The capacitance (C ECW) and resistance (R ECW) of the ECW at each temperature were computed from ΔOD(t) by applying the equivalent-circuit analysis. ΔOD(t) is proportional to charges injected in the ECW: ΔOD(t)=ηQ(t)/S, where η is a coloration efficiency of a WO3 film and S is an active area of the ECW. Q(t) is a function of time,
(5) Q(t)=C ECW V a [1−exp(−t/R ECW C ECW )],
where V a (=−1.5 V) is the applied potential. Therefore,
(6)
ΔOD(t)=− ηC ECW V a [1−exp(−t/R ECW C ECW (] S .
R ECW and C ECW are determined by fitting the experimentally obtained ΔOD(t) using Eq. (6). The EDLC is first charged by applying 1.0 V for 10 min and then discharged under a constant current flow of 1 mA (i 0). On applying a discharge potential across the EDLC, the potential (V) of the EDLC drops step-wise (ΔV 0) at the initial stage of the discharge, and then decreases linearly with time (Δt). A potential gradient (ΔV/Δt) together with a relation of C
carbon =2i 0(Δt/ΔV) yields the capacitance of carbon-based counterelectrode (C carbon). The resistance of the EDLC was determined from the initial potential drop (ΔV 0) using ΔV 0/i 0.

A cyclic voltammogram of the ECW is given in Fig. 3. The main features of the voltammogram are the cathodic current that continues to increase up to −1.5 V and the large anodic peak at about −0.8 V. The cyclic voltammogram closely resembles a cyclic voltammograrn for Li+ intercalation/deintercalation in amorphous WO3 films [9]. This fact indicates that no electrochemical reactions, such as an intercalation/deintercalation reaction or side reactions, occur on the carbon-based counterelectrode, and that it acts as an ion reservoir forming an electric double layer at the carbon-based material/PSE interface.

Fig. 4 shows the ΔOD of the ECW measured at 25°C. The coloring time required to obtain ΔOD=1.0 is 150 s, and the bleaching time is 5 s. The asymmetry of coloring and bleaching time of the ECW is usually observed. This is mainly because the bleaching process is affected by the back emf which accelerates the movement of Li+ [10–12]. Besides, intercalation is largely governed by a potential barrier for the ions to transport at the boundary between a PSE and a WO3 film [13], whereas deintercalation is influenced by ion transport in the film. Deintercalation is then faster than intercalation because of no barrier for the ions to cross.

The electric double layer capacitance of the carbon-based counterelectrocle increases linearly with the covering percentage of carbon dots on an ITO substrate as shown in Fig. 5. The capacitance of the carbon-based counterelectrode follows the relation of C carbon=2.9y (mF cm−2), where y (%) is the covering percentage of carbon dots. The electric double layer capacitance of the ITO substrate is negligibly small compared to that of carbon materials on it, since the intercept of the axis of ordinates corresponding to the capacitance of the ITO substrate itself is virtually 0. Metal oxide films are not expected to have high surface areas. In fact, an electric double layer capacitance of a WO3 film was reported to be between 1 and 20 μF cm−2 [8], and by three orders of magnitude smaller than that of the carbon-based material.

Fig. 6 gives the relationship between ΔODmax and the covering percentage of carbon materials on the ITO substrate. As shown in the figure, ΔODmax of the ECW increases rapidly with the covering percentage of the carbon dots up to a point and levels off for further increase in the covering percentage. According to the simple equivalent-circuit model given in Fig. 2, ΔODmax can be expressed as
(7)
ΔOD max
=−η Q S =−η C WO 3 +C carbon
C WO 3 +C carbon
V a
S ,
where Q is the amount of charge injected in a WO3 film. Since C carbon=2.9y,
(8)
ΔOD max
=−η C WO 3 +2.9y C WO 3 +2.9y
V a
S .

Experimentally obtained ΔODmax was fitted with Eq. (8) by means of least squares method, and the best fitted curve is shown in Fig. 6. The coloration efficiency and the capacitance of the film obtained are, respectively, η=46.2 cm2 C−1 and C WO3 =38 mF cm−2. There are a number of reports on the coloration efficiency of WO3 films. The coloration efficiencies reported depend on deposition conditions and/or methods [14,15] and the present coloration efficiency is a typical value. The capacitance of WO3 films so far reported widely varies from 10 to 100 mF cm−2 [8]. The carbon-based counterelectrode with the covering percentage of 30% yields the capacitance equal to 87 mF cm−2, which is by a factor of 2.3 larger than C WO3 . Therefore, a larger fraction of an applied potential across the ECW goes to a WO3 electrode than to a carbon-based counterelectrode, which in turn results in a higher degree of coloration in the ECW. The advantages of employing a carbon-based electrode as a counterelectrode for the WO, electrode are twofold; (1) a higher electrochemical stability and (2) a higher capacitance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of temperature dependence of electrochromic switching behavior
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 gives response times (τ) of the ECW measured in a temperature region between −20 and 80°C; τ is the time required to obtain the coloration ratio of 4. The response time for coloration decreases exponentially with temperature, and remains nearly constant at higher temperatures. Generally, the response time of an ECW depends on its active-area size, and gets longer with the size. At lower temperatures, however, the decrease in an ionic conductivity is often a dominant factor determining response time, and the ECW active size effect is then weakened. Resistance of the ECW and the EDLC are plotted as a function of temperature in Fig. 8(a). In spite of the difference in the absolute resistance values between the ECW and the EDLC, the temperature dependence of the resistance is quite similar to each other. This is more clearly observed in Fig. 8(b), where the resistances, are normalized by the respective resistance at 20°C. Constituent parts in common with the ECW and the EDLC are the carbon-based electrode and the PSE, and an electric conductivity of the carbon-based electrode is almost independent of temperature in the temperature region studied. Hence, the dominant temperature dependence of resistance in the ECW and the EDLC is concluded to originate chiefly from the temperature dependence of the ionic conductivity of the PSE.

Fig. 9(a) shows the temperature dependence of the capacitance for the ECW and the EDLC. The capacitance of the ECW increases linearly with temperature. The capacitance of the EDLC, on the other hand, increases slightly between −20 and 0°C, and remains nearly constant up to 80°C. Kanbara et al. also studied the temperature dependence of the capacitance of a solid-state EDLC with a poly(vinyl alcohol)-based PSE and an electrolyte solution (GBL/LiClO4(1 M)), and reported the temperature independent electric double layer capacitance between ca. −20 and 70°C [16,17]. The capacitance of the WO3 film (○) and that of the carbon-based counterelectrode (⋅) were estimated with Eqs. (3) and (4), and they are plotted as a function of temperature in Fig. 9(b). The capacitance of the WO3 film monotonically increases with temperature. Habib and Maheswari reported the similar temperature dependence of capacitance in a WO3-Prussian blue electrochromic device [18].

There have been a large number of literatures so far published on electrochromic reactions in WO3 films [8,19,20]. Vuillemin and Bohnke classified an electrochromic process into following four steps to understand kinetics of electrochromic reactions of WO3 films [19].
1.
Step 1: diffusion of Li+ from the bulk of a PSE to the WO3/PSE interface.
2.
Step 2: charge transfer reaction between the Li+ and an electron to produce an intermediate species, i.e. (Li+, e−).
3.
Step 3: diffusion of the intermediate species, (Li+, e−), from the interface into the WO3 layer.
4.
Step 4: redox reaction between (Li+, e−) and a W6+ site leading to a W5+ absorbing site.

They suggested that step 3 and/or step 4 are the rate-determining steps in a coloring process. In the present equivalent-circuit analysis, the diffusion process mentioned in step 3 relates to a part of resistance in a WO3 film. The capacitance of the WO3 film is then governed by a barrier height for the redox reaction between (Li+, e−) and the W6+ site as given in step 4.

We examined the thermal and optical behavior of the ECW fabricated with the carbon based counterelectrode. The electrochemical behavior of the ECWs was found to be explained with the simple equivalent-circuit comprising four constituent parts, two capacitors corresponding to the WO3 film and the carbon-based counterelectrode, the electric resistor of the PSE and the power source connected in series. The capacitance of the carbon-based counterelectrode increases linearly with the covering percentage (y) of the carbon materials in a way of C carbon=2.9y (mF cm−2). The coloration efficiency and the capacitance of the WO3 film were estimated to be η=46.2 cm2 C−1 and C WO3 =38 mF cm−2. The response time for coloration gets longer exponentially with decreasing the temperature of the ECW. This is chiefly due to the temperature dependence of the ionic conductivity for the PSE. The response time of the ECW depends on its active area size and gets longer with the size. At lower temperatures, however, the increase in an ionic conductivity is a dominant factor determining response time, and the size effect is weakened. The capacitance of the WO3 films increases linearly with temperature, whereas that of the carbon-based counterelectrode remains nearly constant in the range of 0–80°C. The capacitance of the WO3 films governed by a barrier height for the redox reaction between (Li+, e−) and the W6+ site as given in step 4.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge Dr. J. Nagai of Asahi Glass Co., Ltd for a useful discussion. We are indebted to Nippon Oil Co., Ltd for permission to publish these results.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: T. Kubo, T. Toya, Y. Nishikitani, J. Nagai, The 193rd Electrochemical Society Meeting Abstracts, San Diego, 98-1, Abstract No. 86 (1998).
T. Kubo, H. Kobayashi, Y. Nishikitani, N. Kuroda, T. Seike, J. Nagai, US Pat. 5,708,523 (1998).
Y. Nishikitani, T. Kubo, N. Kuroda, US Pat. 5,724,176 (1998).
